description.s: A 2D spatial windowing environment that renders attachments as draggable, resizable windows. The primary container view for organizing multiple items visually. Position data lives in each child’s
object — single source of truth, no separate layout table.
Freely positioned via drag handle, corners to resize
Click brings to front; window controls: minimize, maximize, close
Stays in place relative to chosen corner on container resize
Single-axis resize on the non-docked edge
Multiple items visible, persistent layout, direct manipulation
Context switching, single focus, no spatial memory
Spatial organization matches how people naturally arrange information. The complexity cost is worth the UX benefit for desktop workflows.
Why Positioned Objects Over Separate Layout?
Position data could live in a separate layout table, but that creates synchronization issues (child removed but layout entry remains). Embedding position in the attachment
object is a single source of truth.
(edge-anchored) modes. Floating covers general use. Pinned keeps reference panels visible during scrolling. Docked creates IDE-like panel layouts. All three use the same
field — null/unset for floating, corner string for pinned, edge string for docked.

Earlier versions read z-indices from both DOM and database, causing divergence and unbounded growth. The current design treats the database as the single source of truth — z-index functions read fresh attachments from the DB, never from DOM queries. This eliminates an entire class of stacking bugs.

The 2D canvas is desktop-focused. Mobile should use a different container view (e.g., linear list with swipe navigation). Different containers for different contexts is simpler than responsive design.
— how links work in spatial context
For implementation details (z-index layering, scroll preservation, window chrome, architecture), see the documented source code — the view uses rich

so child views can open items as new windows instead of navigating away.
